text: her bag . She shrugged it on and adjusted the straps; it wasn’t terribly heavy, but she’d have to carry it a long way. “Should be easy. Most everyone beds down early .” Ronin nodded, swept his head from side to side in another search, and walked, gravel crunching beneath his boots. Lara adjusted her scarf, fashioning it into a makeshift hood, and followed across the no-man’s land between the wall and the human settlement . It would be easy to forget the danger, now that she was on the other side of the wall again, but they weren’t clear yet. Not until the lights of Cheyenne were a memory, lost beyond the horizon . Quiet apart from their footfalls, they followed the old trail. She’d walked it before, more times than she could count, but it had never felt this way. The last time, she’d been filled with uncertainty and fear. She hadn’t known Ronin when he led her to the Market. Hadn’t known if she’d be okay, if she’d ever find her sister, if she’d survive the night with the strange bot who’d stalked her through the ruins. It was a journey into the unknown . This was, too, but she wasn’t alone now. Ronin was familiar. She loved him, trusted him, would go anywhere with him . Her eyes drifted to her old shack as they neared it. Light spilled through the gaps around the closed door. Oddly, Lara wasn’t upset about someone living there. She and Tabitha had built it, together, but it was never anything more than shelter. Home had been with Tabitha…and now it was with Ronin . “I wonder how long it took .” “How long what took?” Ronin glanced over his shoulder . “For someone to notice I was gone .” He swung his gaze to the shack. “In a way, you’re helping someone else who was in need. Just like Tabitha helped you .” “Yeah,” she said, eyes lingering on the structure while they walked past. When it was behind her, she settled her attention on Ronin’s back . “ Yeah .” They pressed on. Everything was familiar to her, even in the deepening shadows. These were the roads she’d taken to fetch water, to head out to the ruins, to visit Gary and Kate or the other people she sometimes traded with. The few people still outside cast them wary glances. If anyone recognized Lara, no one spoke to her . Soon, the shacks were behind them. Had the place always been so small? Ronin led her up a gradual embankment. At the top, it leveled off on a wide, flat road that stretched as far as she could see to the east and west. The grass and weeds growing through the cracked, mostly-buried pavement swayed in the wind. Only a hint of orange remained on the horizon, now, the rest of the sky black . Ronin stopped at the edge of the road and turned to look north. Lara stood beside him, wind tugging at her scarf and clothing. The coat Ronin gave her blocked the worst of it. She followed his gaze to Cheyenne. The Bot District’s lights were bright, casting a yellow-tinted glow in the hazy sky . “I’m sorry to leave, only because of what that place could have been,” Ronin said . “What do you think it could have been ?” “A place with a promising future. A place worth staying in.” He turned to her, shadows deep on his face. “A place where we wouldn’t have to worry. Where we could just…live. Not just us, but everyone .” Lara took his hand, lacing her fingers with his. “Bots and humans have never lived together .” His hand was warm, fingers firm but not ungentle. “I don’t think that’s true .” She furrowed her brow and gazed up at him. “What do you mean ?” “Our peoples have forgotten, but the way it is in Cheyenne isn’t the way it always was. Something changed it, a long time ago …” “If all those houses are like yours, they have things in them for humans. Toilets and beds and boxes to keep food cold. Humans lived in those houses .” “Yes. And in the Clinic, there are dozens of rooms with signs pertaining to human care .” A strange feeling spread through her, some mix of elation — she’d been right! — and dread. If people had lived in that part of Cheyenne…why didn’t they anymore ? “What about the man who lived in your attic ?” “He probably lived in the house. People lived in all those houses. Until…” He shook his head, and even in the failing light, she saw the tic of his jaw. “Doesn’t matter .” “It does matter,” she insisted. “Until what, Ronin ?” “I will tell you, Lara, but not until there are many miles between us and this place .” “You can tell me now. I deserve to know, don’t I ?” He turned his head and settled his unblinking gaze on her. The wind filled in his silence for a long time . “The journal you found in the attic talks about what happened in Cheyenne, a long time ago. It hints at there having been some terrible war, but the man did not write much about that. He recorded the events unfolding in town. Right outside that window .” Lara squeezed his hand. She thought she knew, somehow, what he was going to tell her, but she wanted to hear it from him . “Warlord was there. I don’t think he was calling himself that yet…but he was there. He forced the humans out of their homes and started building his wall, claiming Cheyenne as his own, and executed those who disobeyed. Many of them in the park .” Her stomach sank, twisting in on itself. All that peaceful grass, the vibrant trees, all grown where people had been murdered ? “The humans rallied together to fight
